title: Dog Sneezing Bloody Mucus: Causes and Veterinary Care
meta_desc: Is your dog sneezing bloody mucus? Learn what causes this symptom, why it is medium urgency, and how a photo can help your veterinarian triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bloody mucus in a dog's sneeze should be evaluated by a vet within 48 hours, but you should seek emergency care immediately if the bleeding is heavy or your dog is lethargic.

What does it mean if my dog has bloody mucus in their sneeze?

  • Bloody mucus, often called epistaxis, occurs when the small, delicate blood vessels within the nasal lining rupture.
  • The discharge can range from a few pink-tinged spots to thick, dark red mucus.
  • Common causes include nasal infections, foreign objects like grass seeds stuck in the nose, dental issues, or environmental irritants that lead to violent sneezing fits.

Is bloody mucus in my dog's sneeze an emergency?

  • Medium Urgency: While a single, minor episode may not be immediately life-threatening, it is not normal and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Emergency Care: You should seek immediate help if the bleeding is profuse, does not stop, or is accompanied by pale gums, lethargy, or difficulty breathing.

How does taking a photo of the discharge help my vet?

  • A clear photo of the discharge and the dog's snout allows the vet to see the exact color, consistency, and volume of the blood.
  • Visual evidence helps the team differentiate between a minor irritation and a more serious internal issue.
  • A photo provides a record of the event if the sneezing has stopped by the time you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, sneezing with bloody mucus can be caused by oronasal neoplasia, dental disease (oronasal fistula), nasal foreign body, or nasopharyngeal disease. Acute rhinitis can also cause nasal discharge that becomes serosanguineous or hemorrhagic if inflammatory cells infiltrate the mucosa. Diagnostic procedures include a complete blood count, serum chemistry profile, urinalysis, coagulation testing (prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, buccal mucosal bleeding time, and platelet count), and imaging of the nasal chambers via radiographs or CT. Rhinoscopy, retroflex nasopharyngoscopy, nasal flushing, and nasal biopsy can be performed after imaging to avoid misinterpreting iatrogenic changes. Nasal biopsies and deep nasal tissue cultures may also be indicated.

Chapter: Otorhinolaryngology, Diagnostic Imaging, Clinical Pathology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1480)

faqs.question: Can allergies cause my dog to sneeze blood?
faqs.answer: While allergies cause inflammation, they rarely cause significant bleeding. However, if a dog sneezes violently and repeatedly due to an allergy, a small blood vessel may occasionally burst.

faqs.question: Should I try to clean my dog's nose?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe the exterior of the nose with a damp cloth, but avoid inserting anything into the nostrils, as this could cause further irritation or worsen the bleeding.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to diagnose the cause?
faqs.answer: The vet may perform a physical exam, check the teeth, or recommend blood tests to check clotting. In some cases, a nasal swab or imaging like an X-ray may be necessary.
